chunk_summary: This section discusses monitoring and managing diuretic-associated electrolyte imbalances in pediatric heart failure, emphasizing safety, regular monitoring, and targeted interventions for hypokalemia, hyponatremia, and metabolic alkalosis.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of monitoring electrolytes in pediatric heart failure patients on diuretics.
Learn the recommended frequency for electrolyte and renal function testing during diuretic therapy.
Identify strategies for correcting hypokalemia and managing metabolic alkalosis in pediatric heart failure.
Recognize the interplay between nutrition and diuretic management in heart failure.
key_facts: Baseline sodium, potassium, bicarbonate, and creatinine should be obtained before starting or adjusting diuretics.
Recheck electrolytes within 48–72 hours after dose changes or addition of a thiazide, or earlier if clinically unstable.
In stable outpatients, monitor every 2–4 weeks until growth and dosing stabilize, then every 2–3 months.
Correct hypokalemia first if the child is symptomatic, prioritizing potassium repletion over calorie escalation.
Persistent hypokalemia may require magnesium assessment, as low magnesium can impair potassium repletion.
Document electrolyte targets and follow-up plans in the nutrition note to integrate feeding and diuretic management.
reference_quote: Diuretic therapy in pediatric heart failure requires operational monitoring for hypovolemia, hypotension, hypokalemia, hyponatremia, metabolic alkalosis, and renal dysfunction, because electrolyte disturbances commonly drive anorexia, weakness, vomiting, and feeding intolerance.
document_key_topics: Electrolyte monitoring in pediatric heart failure
Management of diuretic-associated hypokalemia and hyponatremia
Integration of nutrition and diuretic therapy in heart failure
